en.extract:
The cobalt-rumped parrotlet (Forpus xanthopterygius) or blue-winged parrotlet is a species of parrot in the family Psittacidae. 
There are two subspecies: Salvadori's cobalt-rumped parrotlet (F. x. flavescens) and Hellmayr's cobalt-rumped parrotlet (F. x. flavissimus).


Description


Distribution and habitat
Cobalt-rumped parrotlets are found in much of central and northern South America. Their range is split by each subspecies, as described above. It is generally common and widespread, though more localized in the Amazon Basin.
Cobalt-rumped parrotlets are mainly found in lowlands. They occur in dry and riparian woodlands, cerrado, caatinga, palm groves, semi-arid scrubland, savanna, and pastures. Cobalt-rumped parrotlets tend to avoid densely forested areas. They are not found at altitudes above 1,200 metres (3,900 ft).
Cobalt-rumped parrotlets are generally non-migratory; however, populations in Argentina move locally according to the flowering seasons of various plant species in their diet. Populations in the Brazilian Atlantic Forest are altitudinal migrants.


Conservation
The number of cobalt-rumped parrotlets is unknown, but the population is stable. It is listed as Least Concern by the IUCN Red List. The number of wild individuals is unknown.


Threats
The cobalt-rumped parrotlet is not known to be affected by deforestation or the pet trade, unlike many of its close relatives in the genus Forpus. There are many areas across its range that are already protected.


Behavior and ecology


Social
Flocks are usually around 20 birds but can grow to over 50 around fruiting trees or seeding grasses. They are highly social and gregarious. Cobalt-rumped parrotlets tend to feed in groups, usually between 2 and 12 individuals.
Cobalt-rumped parrotlets call while in flight and perched. Their calls are high-pitched "sheet" or "zeet" screeches or twittering. When feeding and socializing in large groups they make twittering and chattering noises.


Reproduction
Cobalt-rumped parrotlets' breeding season is May to August, but occupied nests have been observed in July, January, and March in different areas of the birds' range. Females lay 3-7 small, white, roughly spherical eggs. Some cobalt-rumped parrotlets have been observed to scrape rufous hornero nests (made of clay) with their beaks. Though the reason for this is not entirely clear, nests created by rufous horneros have been used by cobalt-rumped parrotlets to raise their broods, which has led to the suggestion that the parrotlets are "taste-testing" the nest material to determine whether it is suitable, according to unknown standards, for their needs. However, similar studies of other Forpus species have concluded that clay-licking provides important minerals to the birds that otherwise would not be accessible.


Diet
Cobalt-rumped parrotlets mostly feed on Cecropia sp. and Ficus sp. fruits, Mikania sp. and Trema micrantha seeds, and Ambrosia sp., Handroanthus serratifolius, and Marcgravia sp. flowers. However, they are known to occasionally feed on other plants such as grass. Cobalt-rumped parrotlets have been known to migrate locally based on the flowering and fruiting seasons of some of the main plants of their diet.


Taxonomy
For many years, the cobalt-rumped parrotlet was considered conspecific with the green-rumped parrotlet (Forpus passerinus), but today all authorities recognize the two as separate species. The turquoise-winged parrotlet (F. spengeli) and the riparian parrotlet (F. crassirostris) were also considered conspecific with the cobalt-rumped parrotlet until studies in 2015 pointed out the error in this classification based on morphological and geographical differences. However, some taxonomic authorities, including the American Ornithological Society, still consider them so.
The cobalt-rumped parrotlet, at least until 2021, is a rare case in which the common name has been more stable than the binomial. F. xanthopterygius initially referred to what are now considered two distinct species: the cobalt-rumped parrotlet (then referred to as the blue-winged parrotlet) and the white-winged parakeet (Brotogeris versicolurus). In 1945, the Brazilian ornithologist Olivério Pinto discarded the name F. xanthopterygius for the blue-winged parrotlet and renamed it F. crassirostris (which now applies to the closely related riparian parrotlet). During the same year, the blue-winged parrotlet was mistakenly recorded as F. xanthopterygius crassirostris and was again reverted to F. xanthopterygius. In 1978, Pinto mentioned the mistake in Novo Catálogo das Aves do Brazil and the name was changed to F. crassirostris. However, as was pointed out in 1999, the original name (F. xanthopterygius) remains valid per ICZN rules. Consequently, this name was re-applied to the blue-winged parrotlet. In 2021, the species was renamed from blue-winged parrotlet to cobalt-rumped parrotlet by the IOC, marking the first major change to the common name of the species. Additionally, the name of the nominate subspecies also changed: F. x. xanthopterygius is the subspecies formerly listed as F. crassirostris vividus.


References


External links

Birdphotos.com: map of range
Neotropical Birds by Cornell Lab of Ornithology: species profile and media

The '''cobalt-rumped parrotlet''' ('''''Forpus xanthopterygius''''') or '''blue-winged parrotlet''' is a species of [[parrot]] in the family [[Psittacidae]]. 

There are two subspecies: '''Salvadori's cobalt-rumped parrotlet''' (''F. x. flavescens'') and '''Hellmayr's cobalt-rumped parrotlet''' (''F. x. flavissimus'').<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.parrots.org/encyclopedia/blue-winged-parrotlet|title=Blue-winged Parrotlet (Forpus xanthopterygius) {{!}} Parrot Encyclopedia|website=www.parrots.org|access-date=2019-03-17}}</ref>

==Description==
{| class="wikitable"
|+
!Name
!Appearance
!Range
|-
|'''cobalt-rumped parrotlet'''
(''Forpus xanthopterygius xanthopterygius'') (nominate subspecies)
|Typically around {{Convert|12|cm|in|abbr=}} long and weigh about {{Convert|28|g|oz|abbr=}}. Primarily green or olive green with light peach beaks and feet and dark brown eyes, which are surrounded by bright green. Cobalt-rumped parrotlets are [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]]: males have purple-blue primary, secondary, and underwing coverts with bright blue patch on rump. Females have no blue markings and yellow-green foreheads.<ref name=":0" /> Like all parrots, Cobalt-rumped parrotlets exhibit [[zygodactyly]], meaning two toes face forward and two face backward.
Juveniles look like adults, except males have green feathers mixed in with their blue markings.
|northeastern [[Argentina]] ([[Misiones Province|Misiones]], northeastern Corrientes, eastern [[Chaco Province|Chaco]], eastern [[Formosa Province|Formosa]]), from [[Paraguay]] north through central/mideastern [[Brazil]] to northern [[Bahia]]<ref name=":0" />
|-
|'''Salvadori's cobalt-rumped parrotlet'''
(''F. x. flavescens'')
|Compared to the nominate species, both males and females are more yellow-green or paler in color. Males have paler blue markings on wings and more yellow-green foreheads, cheeks, and underparts.<ref name=":0" />
|mideastern [[Peru]] south to eastern [[Bolivia]] ([[Beni Department|Beni]], [[Santa Cruz Department (Bolivia)|Santa Cruz]])<ref name=":0" />
|-
|'''Hellmayr's cobalt-rumped parrotlet'''
(''F. x. flavissimus'')
|Both males and females are more yellow-green or paler in color. Males have more yellow-green foreheads, cheeks, and throats.<ref name=":0" />
|northeastern [[Brazil]] from [[Maranhão]], [[Ceará]], [[Paraíba|Paraiba]] south to northern [[Bahia]]<ref name=":0" />
|}

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Forpus xanthopterygius -tree hole -Brazil-8.jpg|thumb|right|A male cobalt-rumped parrotlet looking out from a nest in the [[Vale do Ribeira]], Brazil]]Cobalt-rumped parrotlets are found in much of central and northern [[South America]]. Their range is split by each subspecies, as described above. It is generally common and widespread, though more localized in the [[Amazon Basin]].

Cobalt-rumped parrotlets are mainly found in [[lowland]]s. They occur in dry and [[Riparian zone|riparian]] [[woodland]]s, [[cerrado]], [[caatinga]], [[Palm tree|palm groves]], [[Semi-arid climate|semi-arid]] [[scrubland]], [[savanna]], and [[pasture]]s.<ref name=":0" /> Cobalt-rumped parrotlets tend to avoid densely forested areas. They are not found at altitudes above {{Convert|1200|m|ft|abbr=}}.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Parrots: A Guide to Parrots of the World|last1=Parr|first1=M.|last2=Juniper|first2=T.|publisher=A&C Black|year=2010|isbn=978-1-4081-3575-4|pages=176}}</ref>

Cobalt-rumped parrotlets are generally [[Bird migration|non-migratory]]; however, populations in [[Argentina]] move locally according to the flowering seasons of various plant species in their diet. Populations in the Brazilian [[Atlantic Forest]] are [[Altitudinal migration|altitudinal migrants]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/blwpar4/overview|title=Blue-winged Parrotlet - Introduction {{!}} Neotropical Birds Online|website=neotropical.birds.cornell.edu|access-date=2019-03-17}}</ref>

==Conservation==
The number of cobalt-rumped parrotlets is unknown, but the population is stable. It is listed as [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]] by the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> The number of wild individuals is unknown.

===Threats===
The cobalt-rumped parrotlet is not known to be affected by deforestation or the pet trade, unlike many of its close relatives in the genus ''[[Forpus]]''. There are many areas across its range that are already protected.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|title=The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|access-date=2019-03-17}}</ref>

==Behavior and ecology==
[[File:Forpus xanthopterygius -Goias, Brazil -female-8.jpg|left|thumb|A female cobalt-rumped parrotlet in [[Goiás]], Brazil]]
[[File:Forpus xanthopterygius -pets-4.jpg|left|thumb|Pair of cobalt-rumped parrotlets kept as pets]]

===Social===
Flocks are usually around 20 birds but can grow to over 50 around fruiting trees or seeding [[grass]]es. They are highly social and [[gregarious]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://ebird.org/species/blwpar4|title=Blue-winged Parrotlet - eBird|website=ebird.org|language=en|access-date=2019-04-05|archive-date=2019-04-11|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190411133552/https://ebird.org/species/blwpar4/|url-status=dead}}</ref> Cobalt-rumped parrotlets tend to feed in groups, usually between 2 and 12 individuals.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Melo|first1=C.|last2=Oliveira|first2=A.D.|last3=Borges|first3=C.A.|last4=Ribeiro|first4=G.|last5=Tavares|first5=J.|date=November 2009|title=Impact of Forpus xanthopterygius (Spix, 1824) (Aves, Psittacidae) on flowers of Handroanthus serratifolius (Vahl.) S. O. Grose (Bignoniaceae)|journal=Brazilian Journal of Biology|volume=69|issue=4|pages=1149–51|doi=10.1590/S1519-69842009000500020|pmid=19967187|doi-access=free}}</ref>

Cobalt-rumped parrotlets call while in flight and perched. Their calls are high-pitched "sheet" or "zeet" screeches or twittering.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/blue-winged-parrotlet-forpus-xanthopterygius|title=Blue-winged Parrotlet (Forpus xanthopterygius)|website=www.hbw.com|language=en|access-date=2019-04-05}}</ref> When feeding and socializing in large groups they make twittering and chattering noises.<ref name=":0" />

===Reproduction===
Cobalt-rumped parrotlets' breeding season is May to August, but occupied nests have been observed in July, January, and March in different areas of the birds' range.<ref name=":4" /> Females lay 3-7 small, white, roughly spherical eggs.<ref name=":0" /> Some cobalt-rumped parrotlets have been observed to scrape [[rufous hornero]] nests (made of [[clay]]) with their beaks. Though the reason for this is not entirely clear, nests created by rufous horneros have been used by cobalt-rumped parrotlets to raise their broods, which has led to the suggestion that the parrotlets are "taste-testing" the nest material to determine whether it is suitable, according to unknown standards, for their needs.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Sazima|first=I.|date=September 2008|title=The parrotlet Forpus xanthopterygius scrapes at clay nests of the ovenbird Furnarius rufus: tasting or testing a new home?|url=http://repositorio.unicamp.br/bitstream/REPOSIP/72203/1/WOS000208353600012.pdf|journal=Revista Brasileira de Ornitologia|volume=16|pages=256–259|archive-date=2021-01-29|access-date=2019-03-17|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210129235410/http://repositorio.unicamp.br/bitstream/REPOSIP/72203/1/WOS000208353600012.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> However, similar studies of other ''[[Forpus]]'' species have concluded that clay-licking provides important [[mineral]]s to the birds that otherwise would not be accessible.

===Diet===
Cobalt-rumped parrotlets mostly feed on ''[[Cecropia]] sp.'' and ''[[Ficus]] sp.''<ref>{{Cite journal|last=de Figueiredo|first=R.A.|date=1996|title=Vertebrates at neotropical fig species in a forest fragment|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/280578753|journal=Tropical Ecology|publisher=International Society for Tropical Ecology|volume=37|issue=1|pages=139–141|via=ResearchGate}}</ref> fruits, ''[[Mikania]] sp.'' and ''[[Trema micrantha]]'' seeds, and ''[[Ragweed|Ambrosia]] sp., [[Handroanthus serratifolius]]'',<ref name=":2" /> and ''[[Marcgravia]] sp.'' flowers. However, they are known to occasionally feed on other plants such as grass.<ref name=":0" /> Cobalt-rumped parrotlets have been known to migrate locally based on the flowering and fruiting seasons of some of the main plants of their diet.<ref name=":4" />

==Taxonomy==
For many years, the cobalt-rumped parrotlet was considered [[conspecific]] with the [[green-rumped parrotlet]] (''Forpus passerinus''), but today all authorities recognize the two as separate species. The [[turquoise-winged parrotlet]] (''F. spengeli'') and the [[riparian parrotlet]] (''F. crassirostris'') were also considered conspecific with the cobalt-rumped parrotlet until studies in 2015 pointed out the error in this classification based on morphological and geographical differences.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bocalini|first1=F.|last2=Silveira|first2=L.F.|date=May 2015|title=Morphological variability and taxonomy of the Bluewinged Parrotlet Forpus xanthopterygius (Psittacidae)|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/277304595|journal=Revista Brasileira de Ornitologia|volume=23|pages=64–75|via=ResearchGate|doi=10.1007/bf03544290|doi-access=free}}</ref> However, some taxonomic authorities, including the [[American Ornithological Society]], still consider them so.

The cobalt-rumped parrotlet, at least until 2021, is a rare case in which the [[common name]] has been more stable than the [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial]]. ''F. xanthopterygius'' initially referred to what are now considered two distinct species: the cobalt-rumped parrotlet (then referred to as the blue-winged parrotlet) and the [[white-winged parakeet]] (''Brotogeris versicolurus)''. In 1945, the Brazilian [[ornithologist]] [[Olivério Pinto]] discarded the name ''F. xanthopterygius'' for the blue-winged parrotlet and renamed it ''F. crassirostris'' (which now applies to the closely related [[riparian parrotlet]]). During the same year, the blue-winged parrotlet was mistakenly recorded as ''F. xanthopterygius crassirostris'' and was again reverted to ''F. xanthopterygius''. In 1978, Pinto mentioned the mistake in ''Novo Catálogo das Aves do Brazil'' and the name was changed to ''F. crassirostris''. However, as was pointed out in 1999, the original name (''F. xanthopterygius'') remains valid per [[International Code of Zoological Nomenclature|ICZN]] rules. Consequently, this name was re-applied to the blue-winged parrotlet. In 2021, the species was renamed from blue-winged parrotlet to cobalt-rumped parrotlet by the [[International Ornithologists' Union|IOC]], marking the first major change to the common name of the species. Additionally, the name of the [[nominate subspecies]] also changed: ''F. x. xanthopterygius'' is the subspecies formerly listed as ''F. crassirostris vividus''.
